nativeRange.habitat: Tropical coral reef slopes and lagoons, where individuals hover above live rock and forage for zooplankton.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with crevices and overhangs

diet.notes: Tends to be a mid-water planktivore in the wild but adapts readily to captive feeding. Generally advised to offer a varied mix of mysis, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and herbivore preparations. High metabolism — small feedings 2–3 times per day are commonly recommended.

goodWith: most peaceful and semi-aggressive reef tankmates
avoidWith: other Meiacanthus species in the same tank
very large or boisterous predators that may stress or harass it

summary: The Canary Blenny is a vivid yellow fang blenny endemic to Fiji, generally considered reef-safe and compatible with most peaceful tankmates. Captive-bred specimens are widely available. Like all Meiacanthus blennies, it carries venomous fangs in its lower jaw used as a defensive bite.
careNotes:
VENOMOUS FANGS: Meiacanthus oualanensis and its congeners possess two enlarged, grooved canines in the lower jaw connected to venom glands; the venom is delivered via a defensive bite rather than through spines, and has been reported to feel similar to a bee or wasp sting. Bites to humans are not lethal but are painful — hand-feeding and careless tank maintenance are strongly discouraged. The venom deters most predators in the wild, and several non-venomous mimic species — including Petroscirtes breviceps — imitate Meiacanthus coloration as a result.

Captive-bred specimens are widely available and tend to adapt readily to prepared foods, making the Canary Blenny one of the more approachable marine blennies for an intermediate keeper. It is best kept singly, or as a confirmed mated pair, in any given tank — individuals will not tolerate conspecifics or other Meiacanthus, but are otherwise peaceful toward unrelated tankmates and are generally considered reef-safe with corals, clams, and ornamental shrimp.

Provide mature live rock with crevices for retreat, along with open mid-water swimming space. The Canary Blenny tends to hover and dart in the water column rather than rest on the substrate. It has been known to jump when startled or harassed, so a tight-fitting lid is strongly recommended.
breedingNotes: The Canary Blenny has been bred in captivity at commercial scale and captive-bred specimens are widely available through the aquarium trade. Pairs tend to form readily when a male and female are introduced together while young. Males will defend small caves or crevices where females deposit adhesive, demersal egg clutches (eggs that stick to a surface rather than drifting freely). The eggs hatch into pelagic larvae — free-floating juveniles that live in the water column — which require small live foods such as rotifers and copepod nauplii to survive. Breeding the species is generally considered moderately difficult: less demanding than many marine fish, but the larval stage still requires dedicated rearing infrastructure and some experience with live-food cultures.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk
additional.notes: Provided 10 cm adult size figure and 23–27°C temperature range. Note: source incorrectly lists 'Western Indian Ocean' as the range; the species is actually Fiji-endemic (Western Central Pacific) per FishBase.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority. Family Blenniidae, Order Blenniiformes, max length 10 cm SL, distribution Western Central Pacific (Fiji), IUCN Least Concern. Cross-checked secondary as Reef2Reef wiki returned 403; substituted FishBase per playbook fallback rule.

fish.breedingNotes: Pair-spawning species. Males defend small caves where females deposit demersal egg clutches; eggs hatch into pelagic larvae requiring rotifers and copepod nauplii. Captive-bred at commercial scale.
